did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), a deep learning algorithm, to classify basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in ex vivo confocal microscopy (EVCM) images. The study trained CNN models on EVCM, H&E, and combined datasets to detect BCC in freshly excised tissues from Mohs surgery patients.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document explicitly states 'Article Type: Original article' on page 2. It presents original experimental results, including the development and testing of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) on specific datasets of ex vivo confocal microscopy images, with detailed Methods, Results, and Discussion sections.

title: CLASSIFICATION OF BASAL CELL CARCINOMA IN EX VIVO CONFOCAL MICROSCOPY IMAGES FROM FRESHLY EXCISED TISSUES USING A DEEP LEARNING ALGORITHM
brief_summary: This study developed and evaluated Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to automatically detect basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in ex vivo confocal microscopy (EVCM) images obtained from freshly excised tissues during Mohs surgery. The researchers created three training datasets comprising EVCM images, H&E images, and a combination of both, using samples collected from Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center. Model performance was assessed on a separate test set of 107 EVCM images from 11 patients. The CNN trained on EVCM images achieved 92% diagnostic accuracy and an AUC of 0.94, while the model trained on H&E images achieved 93% accuracy and an AUC of 0.95. The study concludes that deep learning algorithms can accurately classify BCC in EVCM images, with H&E-trained models showing comparable performance to EVCM-trained ones, suggesting potential for aiding real-time diagnosis in surgical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the classification task. The study included 47 BCCs in the training set (18 nodular, 11 superficial, 10 infiltrative, 8 mix-subtype) and 7 BCCs in the test set. The paper notes that pigmented BCCs were not included in the analysis.

The negative class used for binary classification. The study included 28 negative samples in the training set and 4 negative samples in the test set. These samples contained normal skin structures such as sebaceous glands, hair follicles, epidermis, and eccrine ducts.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic classification of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) vs. not-BCC (negative/normal skin tissue)
all_task_types.details: The study performs binary classification to detect the presence or absence of BCC in ex vivo confocal microscopy (EVCM) images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories ('BCC' or 'not-BCC') using Convolutional Neural Networks (ResNet50). Performance was evaluated using diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive/negative predictive values, and Area Under the Curve (AUC) for diagnostic dichotomous classification.

datasets_used.name: EVCM Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of digitally colored purple and pink ex vivo confocal microscopy (EVCM) images obtained from freshly excised tissues from patients undergoing Mohs surgery at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center. Images were acquired using a Vivascope 2500 device after dipping tissues in acridine orange dye.

use.training: Used to train a ResNet50 CNN model for BCC detection.

datasets_used.name: H&E Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of routine histopathology H&E stained slides retrieved from the Department of Dermatopathology at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center. Slides were digitized using an Aperio AT2 slide Scanner.

use.training: Used to train a ResNet50 CNN model for BCC detection.

datasets_used.name: Combined EVCM and H&E Training Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset created by combining all images from the EVCM training dataset and the H&E training dataset.

use.training: Used to train a ResNet50 CNN model for BCC detection.

datasets_used.name: EVCM Test Dataset
datasets_used.description: A separate test set of EVCM images obtained from 7 BCCs and 4 negative normal skin tissue samples from 11 patients. These samples were not previously shown to the algorithm. Images were acquired from freshly discarded surgical margins from Mohs surgery.

use.testing: Used to evaluate and compare the performance of the three trained CNN models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the ability of the CNN models to discriminate between 'BCC' and 'not-BCC' classes.
To estimate uncertainty for the Area Under the Curve (AUC) and for sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, and accuracy measures.
To generate ROC curves and calculate 95% confidence intervals.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Statistical analysis was limited to calculating diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive/negative predictive values, and Area Under the Curve (AUC) with 95% confidence intervals using the pROC package in R.

EVCM Test Dataset.description: Test set of 107 EVCM images (45 BCC, 62 not-BCC) from 11 patients, not used in training.
EVCM Test Dataset.notes: The EVCM model had higher sensitivity but lower specificity compared to the H&E model. The combined model had the lowest diagnostic accuracy and sensitivity but high specificity similar to the H&E model. The H&E model achieved the best overall accuracy.

training_details.training_schedule: Training and validation subsets used for training; testing subset used for evaluation to avoid overfitting

classification_or_output_head.details: Output rendered as diagnosis of images

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 300x300 pixels to speed up model training and reduce complexity
Data augmentation via synthetic copy creation using random rotation, shifting, and resizing to balance class distribution (increasing to 10,000 images per class)
Gradient map generation using Grad-CAM for visual explanation of CNN predictions

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Synthetic copy creation via geometric transforms
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Synthetic copies were created by applying random rotation, shifting, and resizing to balance class distribution, increasing the total number of images to 10,000 per class.
All input images were resized to 300x300 pixels to speed up model training time and reduce model complexity.

conclusions: A deep learning algorithm (ResNet50 CNN) can accurately classify basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in ex vivo confocal microscopy (EVCM) images, achieving diagnostic accuracy comparable to that of dermatologists.
The model trained on EVCM images achieved 92% diagnostic accuracy and an AUC of 0.94, while the model trained on H&E images achieved 93% accuracy and an AUC of 0.95.
A CNN model trained exclusively on H&E images can accurately detect BCC in EVCM images, suggesting that the more readily available H&E dataset can be used to train models for EVCM diagnosis.

The combined model (trained on both EVCM and H&E images) had the lowest diagnostic accuracy (86%) and sensitivity (78%) compared to the EVCM-only and H&E-only models, despite having high specificity (92%).
The study acknowledges limitations including a small sample size for each BCC subtype, the exclusion of pigmented BCCs, and the lack of testing in actual clinical practice.
Results were derived from a single institution, so the findings require validation in multi-centric studies to account for variability in staining protocols and tissue processing.
Future work should include prospective and larger-scale studies to validate the technology in real clinical practice and potentially explore weakly supervised training methods.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small sample size for each BCC subtype, which may not represent all morphological appearances of BCCs encountered in clinical practice.
Pigmented basal cell carcinomas were excluded from the analysis, limiting the generalizability of the findings to this specific subtype.

All images were acquired from a single institution (Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center), which does not account for variability in staining protocols and tissue processing across different centers; multi-centric validation is required.
The algorithm has not yet been tested in actual clinical practice or real-time surgical settings.
The test set consisted of freshly discarded surgical margins, which typically have a lower tumor burden than central tumor de-bulk tissue; performance on de-bulk specimens may differ.
The combined model (trained on both EVCM and H&E images) exhibited the lowest diagnostic accuracy (86%) and sensitivity (78%) among the three models, despite high specificity.

The H&E-trained model achieved comparable performance to the EVCM-trained model, suggesting that readily available H&E datasets can be used to train models for EVCM diagnosis, though this relies on the assumption that H&E features translate effectively to EVCM image characteristics.
